Zechariah 12 presents a vision so strange and troubling that we often skip past it without truly feeling its weight. The prophet speaks of Jerusalem becoming a "cup of drunkenness" to the nations—an image of intoxication, confusion, and loss of control. God promises that when all the earth gathers against this city, He will make it unmovable, a stone that injures all who try to lift it. The military language is stark: panic struck into horses, madness into riders, blindness poured out on enemy forces. And yet beneath this apocalyptic warfare runs an unexpected current. The leaders of Judah will recognize that their strength lies not in military strategy but in the reality that "the LORD of Hosts is their God." The weakest among Jerusalem will be elevated to David's strength; David's house will be like God Himself. This is not the language of ordinary deliverance. It is the language of divine transformation.

But the vision takes a turn that arrests us. In the midst of salvation and victory, God promises to pour out "a spirit of grace and prayer" on Jerusalem—and then something remarkable happens. They will look upon "the One they have pierced" and mourn for Him as for an only child, a firstborn son. The text does not explain who this pierced One is. It simply states it, leaving the reality suspended in the prophecy like light through stained glass. The mourning that follows is not the mourning of defeat but of grief so deep it shakes the land. Every family grieves—the house of David, the house of Nathan, the house of Levi, the house of Shimei, and all remaining clans. This is corporate repentance, a wailing as profound as the legendary lamentation of Hadad-rimmon. The passage does not soften this moment or rush past it. It dwells in the sorrow.

What makes this prophecy so disorienting is that victory and piercing, salvation and grief, deliverance and mourning, exist in the same vision. God saves Jerusalem from external enemies, yet His salvation includes the revelation of an internal tragedy—that they have pierced One who deserves their love. The New Testament eventually illuminates this mystery: it is Jesus Christ, the eternal Son, whose piercing at Calvary becomes the doorway through which Israel's true salvation enters. Yet Zechariah does not resolve the tension. He leaves it there, asking us to hold both realities: God's fierce protection of His people and His people's unfathomable rejection of the One through whom that protection ultimately comes. The vision calls us to see that true deliverance always involves the confrontation with our sin, and true mourning always opens the way to true grace.
